title: Operating System Bugs and Software Misconfigurations
description: Learn how software vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, weak access controls, and social engineering can allow attackers to gain privileges they were never intended to have.

attack_stages.title: Initial Access
attack_stages.description: The attacker gains access to a standard employee account.

attack_stages.title: Identify Weakness
attack_stages.description: The attacker discovers software vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, or weak access controls.

attack_stages.title: Privilege Escalation
attack_stages.description: The attacker expands or elevates their privileges within the system.

attack_stages.title: System Control
attack_stages.description: The attacker gains unauthorized control over protected resources.

attack_stages.title: Business Impact
attack_stages.description: Sensitive information and critical services are compromised.

prevention_points.title: Keep Software Updated
prevention_points.description: Regularly patch operating systems and applications to eliminate known vulnerabilities.

prevention_points.title: Review System Configurations
prevention_points.description: Remove unnecessary permissions and secure administrative interfaces.

prevention_points.title: Apply the Principle of Least Privilege
prevention_points.description: Users should only have the permissions required for their jobs.

prevention_points.title: Monitor Privileged Activity
prevention_points.description: Continuously monitor administrator actions and investigate unusual behavior.

nodes.title: Scenario
nodes.text: A company stores sensitive employee information in an internal database. Every employee has a standard user account to perform daily tasks. An attacker has successfully compromised one employee's account.

nodes.title: What is Privilege Escalation?
nodes.text: Privilege escalation occurs when an attacker gains permissions or access that were never intended by the system administrator. The objective is to perform actions reserved for more privileged users, such as executing administrative commands, accessing protected information, modifying system settings, or controlling critical resources.

nodes.title: Common Weaknesses
nodes.text: Attackers search for operating system bugs, vulnerable software, buffer overflows, injection flaws, overly permissive file permissions, exposed administrative interfaces, weak access controls, or employees who can be manipulated through social engineering. Any of these weaknesses can provide an opportunity to increase their privileges.

nodes.title: Attacker Goal
nodes.text: The attacker already has access to one employee account. Their next objective is to obtain additional privileges so they can access confidential information or take control of the system.

nodes.title: Which action could an attacker attempt next?
Horizontal Privilege Escalation
nodes.text: Horizontal privilege escalation occurs when an attacker gains access to another user's account while remaining at the same privilege level. Instead of becoming an administrator, they impersonate another employee with similar permissions. Attackers commonly achieve this through session hijacking, stolen credentials, phishing, malware, or credential reuse.

nodes.title: Vertical Privilege Escalation
nodes.text: Vertical privilege escalation occurs when an attacker increases their permission level—for example, moving from a standard employee account to a system administrator. This is commonly achieved by exploiting operating system vulnerabilities, software bugs, insecure configurations, exposed administrative services, or applications running with excessive permissions.

nodes.title: How Attackers Achieve Privilege Escalation
nodes.text: Attackers often combine technical vulnerabilities with human error. They may exploit vulnerable software, abuse weak configurations, or manipulate employees into revealing credentials, downloading malware disguised as legitimate files, or granting access under false pretenses. Once inside the system, they search for opportunities to increase or expand their privileges.

nodes.title: Administrator Access
nodes.text: After successfully escalating privileges, the attacker can execute administrative commands, create new privileged accounts, disable security controls, modify configurations, access protected files, and control sensitive database resources.

nodes.title: Business Impact
nodes.text: Privilege escalation can result in unauthorized access to confidential information, modification or deletion of critical data, installation of persistent malware, disruption of business operations, and long-term compromise of organizational systems.

nodes.title: How to Prevent This
nodes.text: Organizations should regularly patch operating systems and applications, audit user roles and permissions, follow the principle of least privilege, secure administrative interfaces, monitor privileged activity, and educate employees to recognize phishing and other social engineering attacks.

nodes.title: Attack Replay
nodes.text: Review the path the attacker followed from an ordinary employee account to privileged access. Notice each point where security controls could have interrupted the attack.

nodes.title: Scenario Complete
nodes.text: Privilege escalation rarely happens because of a single weakness. It is usually the result of vulnerable software, insecure configurations, weak access controls, and human error working together. Applying layered security controls significantly reduces the likelihood of attackers successfully escalating their privileges.
